The Hidden Burden: Girls, Chores & Survival

A data story on gender inequality among children worldwide — UNICEF Data

Author

Théo Illy

Code 
import polars as pl
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import numpy as np
from plotnine import *
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

Data Overview

Before diving into the story, here is a quick exploration of the two datasets used in this analysis.

Code 
indicator1 = pl.read_csv("/content/drive/MyDrive/unicef_data/unicef_indicator_1.csv")
indicator2 = pl.read_csv("/content/drive/MyDrive/unicef_data/unicef_indicator_2.csv")
Code 
summary = (
    indicator1
    .group_by("sex")
    .agg([
        pl.col("obs_value").mean().round(1).alias("Mean hours/week"),
        pl.col("obs_value").min().round(1).alias("Min"),
        pl.col("obs_value").max().round(1).alias("Max")
    ])
    .sort("sex")
)

print("Chore hours by sex (indicator 1):")
print(summary)

print(f"\nIndicator 1: {indicator1['country'].n_unique()} countries, {indicator1['time_period'].min()}–{indicator1['time_period'].max()}")
print(f"Indicator 2: {indicator2['country'].n_unique()} countries, {indicator2['time_period'].min()}–{indicator2['time_period'].max()}")
print(f"\nGirls spend on average {round(indicator1.filter(pl.col('sex')=='Female')['obs_value'].mean(),1)} hrs/week on chores vs {round(indicator1.filter(pl.col('sex')=='Male')['obs_value'].mean(),1)} hrs/week for boys.")
Chore hours by sex (indicator 1):
shape: (3, 4)
┌────────┬─────────────────┬─────┬──────┐
│ sex    ┆ Mean hours/week ┆ Min ┆ Max  │
│ ---    ┆ ---             ┆ --- ┆ ---  │
│ str    ┆ f64             ┆ f64 ┆ f64  │
╞════════╪═════════════════╪═════╪══════╡
│ Female ┆ 10.0            ┆ 1.4 ┆ 29.7 │
│ Male   ┆ 7.5             ┆ 1.1 ┆ 23.9 │
│ Total  ┆ 8.8             ┆ 1.2 ┆ 27.2 │
└────────┴─────────────────┴─────┴──────┘

Indicator 1: 85 countries, 2005–2020
Indicator 2: 199 countries, 1990–2023

Girls spend on average 10.0 hrs/week on chores vs 7.5 hrs/week for boys.

The Invisible Inequality

Across much of the world, inequality between boys and girls begins long before adulthood. It starts in childhood, inside the home. In countries such as Chad, Yemen, and Somalia, girls aged 10 to 14 spend up to 30 hours per week on household chores — often fetching water, cooking, or caring for siblings. This is nearly twice the time spent by boys, creating an early divide in how time, energy, and opportunities are distributed.

What appears to be routine domestic work is, in reality, a powerful constraint. Time spent on chores is time not spent in school, at rest, or building skills. Though largely invisible in economic statistics, this burden is deeply embedded in daily life, and it sets the stage for broader gender inequality that extends far beyond childhood.

Code 
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.cm as cm
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors

world = gpd.read_file("https://naciscdn.org/naturalearth/110m/cultural/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.zip")

chore_female = (
    indicator1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Female")
    .group_by("country", "alpha_3_code")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("avg_chore_hours"))
    .to_pandas()
)

world_merged = world.merge(chore_female, left_on="ADM0_A3", right_on="alpha_3_code", how="left")
world_merged = world_merged[world_merged["ADM0_A3"] != "ATA"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(12, 6))
world_merged[world_merged["avg_chore_hours"].isna()].plot(ax=ax, color="#d0d0d0", edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3)
world_merged[world_merged["avg_chore_hours"].notna()].plot(
    column="avg_chore_hours",
    ax=ax,
    cmap="YlOrRd",
    legend=True,
    legend_kwds={"label": "Avg hours/week", "shrink": 0.5},
    edgecolor="white",
    linewidth=0.3
)
ax.set_title("The Gender Gap in Household Chores:\nHow Many Hours Do Girls Work Each Week?",
             fontsize=14, fontweight="bold", pad=15)
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("worldmap.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

The Gender Gap in Chores: Girls Work More, Everywhere

The map above reveals a stark geographic pattern: the heaviest burdens are concentrated in sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia. But the gap is not just about geography — it is fundamentally about gender.

The countries where female youth mortality is highest are not randomly distributed. They overlap almost perfectly with those where girls carry the greatest domestic workload.

Code 
# Filter female mortality 2015-2023, average per country
top20_mortality = (
    indicator2
    .filter(
        (pl.col("sex") == "Female") &
        (pl.col("time_period") >= 2015) &
        (pl.col("time_period") <= 2023)
    )
    .group_by("country")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("avg_mortality"))
    .sort("avg_mortality", descending=True)
    .head(20)
    .to_pandas()
)

(
    ggplot(top20_mortality, aes(x="reorder(country, avg_mortality)", y="avg_mortality"))
    + geom_col(fill="#c0392b", width=0.75)
    + coord_flip()
    + labs(
        title="Countries with Highest Female Youth Mortality (2015–2023)",
        x="",
        y="Deaths per 1,000 female youths"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=10),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank()
    )
)

When Inequality Becomes Risk

Beyond its social implications, the data reveals a more alarming reality: countries where girls carry the heaviest domestic workload are also those where young women are most at risk of dying.

In Central African Republic, Sierra Leone, and Chad, where girls’ chore time is particularly high, female youth mortality rates exceed 100 deaths per 1,000 — among the highest in the world. Similarly, countries like Nigeria, Guinea, and Angola show a combination of both high domestic burden and elevated mortality risks.

The scatterplot below makes this link undeniable.

Code 
# Female chore hours — average per country
chore_avg = (
    indicator1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Female")
    .group_by("country")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("chore_hours"))
)

# Female mortality 2015-2023 — average per country
mortality_avg = (
    indicator2
    .filter(
        (pl.col("sex") == "Female") &
        (pl.col("time_period") >= 2015) &
        (pl.col("time_period") <= 2023)
    )
    .group_by("country")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("mortality_rate"))
)

# Join both datasets on country
scatter_df = chore_avg.join(mortality_avg, on="country", how="inner").to_pandas()

# Identify notable countries to label
notable = ["Central African Republic", "Somalia", "Sierra Leone", "Chad",
           "Afghanistan", "Guinea", "Angola", "Sudan", "Yemen", "Cameroon"]
scatter_df["label"] = scatter_df["country"].where(scatter_df["country"].isin(notable), "")

(
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x="chore_hours", y="mortality_rate"))
    + geom_point(aes(color="mortality_rate"), alpha=0.7, size=3)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="#2c3e50", se=True, linetype="solid")
    + geom_text(aes(label="label"), size=7, nudge_y=1.5, nudge_x=0.2, color="#2c3e50")
    + scale_color_gradient(low="#f9c784", high="#c0392b", name="Mortality rate")
    + labs(
        title="Where Girls Do More Chores, Young Women Die More",
        x="Girls' average hours of chores per week",
        y="Female youth mortality rate (2015–2023)"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        legend_position="right"
    )
)

Progress Made — But Unevenly

A correlation of 0.55 between girls’ time spent on chores and female youth mortality suggests a strong and meaningful link. The data does not just show correlation — it reveals a pattern of compounded vulnerability, where inequality at home translates into life-or-death outcomes.

In practical terms, girls who spend long hours on chores are more likely to miss school, face early marriage, or lack access to essential healthcare. In countries like Afghanistan or Sudan, where both gender inequality and mortality remain high, these overlapping disadvantages create a cycle that is difficult to break.

Yet there is reason for cautious hope. Global youth mortality has declined significantly since 1990.

Code 
# Global average mortality per year and sex (excluding Total)
time_series = (
    indicator2
    .filter(pl.col("sex").is_in(["Female", "Male"]))
    .group_by("time_period", "sex")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("avg_mortality"))
    .sort("time_period")
    .to_pandas()
)

(
    ggplot(time_series, aes(x="time_period", y="avg_mortality", color="sex"))
    + geom_line(size=1.2)
    + geom_point(size=2, alpha=0.6)
    + scale_color_manual(values={"Female": "#c0392b", "Male": "#e67e22"}, name="Sex")
    + labs(
        title="Youth Mortality (15–24) Has Halved Since 1990 — But Gaps Remain",
        x="Year",
        y="Average deaths per 1,000 youths"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        legend_position="right"
    )
    + annotate("text", x=1993, y=time_series["avg_mortality"].max() * 0.95,
               label="Progress since 1990", size=9, color="#555555")
)

A Call to Act

The burden placed on girls is invisible in policy discussions, yet visible in the data. Where gender inequality begins in the home, it ends in preventable deaths. Addressing the disproportionate workload imposed on girls is not only a matter of fairness — it is a public health imperative. The world has made progress, but the data shows we are far from done.

Key takeaways from this analysis:

  • Girls aged 10–14 spend on average nearly twice as many hours on chores as boys
  • The countries with the highest female chore burden are also those with the highest female youth mortality
  • Global youth mortality has declined by more than 50% since 1990, but progress is uneven
  • A correlation of 0.55 between chore hours and mortality underlines the systemic nature of this inequality

Every child deserves a fair chance. Support UNICEF’s work to end gender inequality.
